title: How to Get Database Shared Network Right Before Your Server Scales
author: Veltrix
excerpt: A practical breakdown of the Veltrix configuration layer that determines whether your server scales cleanly or stalls at the first growth inflection point.

content.text: The immediate need to lock down database sharing drives Hytale operators to Veltrix configuration after deployment, rather than at planning. When a server begins traffic, the database.yml file can suddenly exhibit connection limits, PoolSize conflicts, or timeout thresholds that spike under multithreaded world updates. Operators initially set defaults that satisfy a handful of players, but
What Operators Are Actually Searching For
Operators initially set defaults that satisfy a handful of players, but
The Architecture Decisions That Determine How Well You Scale

The Architecture Decisions That Determine How Well You Scale   Veltrix’s core configuration sits inside a handful of YAML files that dictate how each Hytale node talks to the shared database, each other, and to the player base. In proxy-config.yml the max_concurrent_connections, routing_mode, and connection_timeout_ms values must reflect the expected peak player pressure; biasing this value too low forces stickiness and back‑pressure, while too high can flood the downstream database. In database.yml the pool_size and max_retry_count need to align with the same arithmetic; a pool of two when a shard demands eight selected workers stalls all streaming data, turning a smooth login into a repeated 5‑second blind dance. The sync-rules.yml sync_interval_seconds parameter underscores how economy and progression are kept coherent; a slow interval can fragment item ownership across nodes, resulting in “ghost” items that players fight over. Finally, health-monitor.yml’s alert_threshold and check_interval_secs set the sensitivity of the alerting stack; if the threshold sits too high against a 30‑second interval, go‑to bans go unnoticed and players experience stale worlds, breeding frustration rather than expansion.
Database and Proxy: Getting the Foundation Right

Database and Proxy: Getting the Foundation Right   The first layer of the Veltrix stack is the shared database defined in database.yml. This file introduces pool_size, connection_timeout_ms, ssl_mode, and eviction_policy. Mis‑setting pool_size below the concurrent player count, or trimming connection_timeout_ms to a value that the motion engine interprets as a hard stop, will trigger time‑out events that freeze inventories and damage progression. Bypassing the pool completely by disabling eviction_policy leaves a skeletal snapshot that happily grows but runs out of memory, drawing a nonexistent bottleneck that the proxy will try to bear. Proxy-config.yml blends the ATM strategy and retry_attempts; an overly aggressive retry_attempts can cause the proxy to spin up duplicate session streams, blowing the database pool. Setting routing_mode to FAIL_SAFE when the deployment rotates shards will break economy sync, causing duplicate trades. When sync-rules.yml’s conflict_resolution rules are too permissive, simultaneous updates colliding across the network become a maintenance nightmare. In short, if any of those parameters do not reflect the real traffic budget, the entire server collapse is stitched through latency, corrupted state, and accidental aborts.

A sharp insight: in Veltrix the proxy-config max_concurrent_connections must stay within ±15% of the database pool_size at a 2 000‑player surge; otherwise, silent transaction stalls trim latency every 0.7 sec.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

Among the most common errors operators make when setting up a Veltrix network is mis‑routing in the proxy-config.yml. Leaving the “destination” node without a fully qualified host or missing the “max_connections” parameter can cause players to reach a dead‑end or see downtimes when the proxy queue overflows, leading to abrupt disconnects during high‑traffic raids. Another frequent mistake occurs in database.yml, where operators accidentally set “max_pool_size” too low; this throttles new player joins and creates a build‑up of stale connections that stutter the economy thread in sync-rules.yml. A third pitfall is forgetting the “cross_server_economy” flag in sync-rules.yml; the resulting desynchronization of in‑game currency across VX‑NET‑01 and server clusters leads to phantom inventories and players reporting double‑charged items. Finally, the health-monitor.yml often has its “critical_latency_ms” threshold set too aggressively; a 20‑ms window can trigger alarm cascades for perfectly normal traffic, forcing operators to, unnecessarily, reboot both the analytics dashboard on VX‑NET‑02 and the backup daemon on VX‑NET‑03, thereby destabilizing the very stability they try to preserve.
Questions Operators Are Asking Right Now

Addressing the operator questions from a Veltrix standpoint, sharing a Hytale server with a network of friends is accomplished by editing proxy-config.yml to forward client ports to an instance’s internal address and binding that instance to VX‑NET‑01’s economy bridge. This bridge guarantees that every transaction crossing server limits is reflected instantly thanks to the sync-rules.yml cross‑server logic. While the end‑user experience hides all of the underlying transport detail, Veltrix itself relies on a TCP‑based socket layer to guarantee reliable delivery of the custom Hytale protocol, ensuring that position updates and inventory changes are never lost. Hosting a Hytale server in Veltrix therefore starts with allocating a dedicated virtual machine, configuring database.yml for connection pooling, linking that instance to the multi‑network bridge, and finally enabling health-monitor.yml to watch key metrics so the operator can react before a scaling event leads to loss of player sessions.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

The correct implementation sequence for Veltrix network configuration, as recommended, begins with Step 1: create a clean proxy-config.yml that specifies the explicit route and latency limits for each target server. Step 2: populate database.yml with the appropriate host, port, user, and set a conservative max_pool_size that matches expected peak loads, thereby preventing connection starvation. Step 3: tailor sync-rules.yml to enable cross‑server economy sharing and define the delay window for progression updates, ensuring consistency across VX‑NET‑01 and any added servers. Step 4: adjust health-monitor.yml with thresholds for CPU, memory, and latency that reflect realistic operating conditions, allowing the server analytics dashboard on VX‑NET‑02 to surface meaningful alerts. Following this order guarantees that each component is supported by the next, creating a robust infrastructure ready for scaling.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Veltrix’s core configuration hinges on a handful of YAML parameters that steer performance once player counts climb. In the database.yml, pool_size is the top‑priority knob; with 10–20 players, a pool of 8 connections suffices, while moving to 200 players demands a pool of 32 to keep latency under 20 ms. The associated timeout, set to 30 s for light loads, should be tightened to 10 s for heavier traffic to prevent stale connections. The max_connections setting must exceed concurrent player attempts: 16 for 10–50 players, 64 for 50–200, and 256 for 200+ to guard against connection saturation. In proxy-config.yml, routing_strategy should shift from single‑node to round‑robin once player count passes 100, and connection_pooling must be activated at 150. The sync-rules.yml’s sync_interval is critical: 60 s for 10–50 players, 30 s for 50–200, and 10 s for 200+ ensures evolved progression and economy ties do not stutter. Health‑monitor.yml’s alert_cpu_threshold starts at 75 % and 70 % for 10–200 players, tightening to 60 % for larger rosters, while alert_mem_threshold moves from 70 % down to 55 % as memory pressure grows. These ordered parameters represent the natural progression from local single‑server stability to distributed scalability.
At scale, Veltrix’s default sync-rules.yml sync_interval can lock the entire shared economy, causing data races and lag spikes.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

When a Hytale server’s player base expands beyond 200, Veltrix’s configuration must evolve across multiple layers. First, database.yml’s pool_size should be raised to 128 or more, matching the increased concurrent read/write demands, and timeout values should be reduced to a few seconds to quickly recycle idle connections. In proxy-config.yml, enable multi‑region routing and increase the routing_strategy threshold to leverage load‑balancing across the VX‑NET‑01 bridge nodes; this distributes traffic evenly and reduces single‑point bottlenecks. The sync-rules.yml sync_interval must be shortened to 5–10 seconds to keep cross‑server economy and progression tightly coupled, while conflict_resolution settings should be tuned to an optimistic concurrency approach to prevent write stalls. Health‑monitor.yml alert thresholds need to be lowered: CPU alerts to 65 % and memory to 60 % for 200+ players, ensuring early detection of resource strain. Finally, enable the Automatic Backup System VX‑NET‑03 to run more frequent snapshots, and configure the Server Analytics Dashboard VX‑NET‑02 to aggregate metrics at a higher resolution, providing real‑time insights into scaling issues and guiding future parameter adjustments.
Related Topics Worth Understanding

Hytale server hosting emerges as a foundational step for operators looking to deploy a stable first‑party experience, and understanding its interplay with Veltrix is crucial. The phrase “Hytale server hosting” signals that the operator is focused on configuring the game’s core services—launcher launch, world loading, and player synchronization—within a Veltrix environment. When the query “Hytale server database shared network not working” surfaces, it indicates that the database.yml is misconfigured, preventing seamless cross‑server data exchange such as shared economy state or progression metrics. This failure often manifests as lagging currency balances or desynced achievements. Finally, a generic “Hytale servers” search reflects broader operational concerns: ensuring that VK‑API endpoints, multiserver clustering, and user authentication remain uninterrupted as the network scales. Each search term highlights a distinct layer of Veltrix configuration that requires attention before growth.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before any launch, every Veltrix prerequisite must be verified. The proxy-config.yml must whitelist all intended server node IPs and assign correct routing tokens so that player traffic is correctly bifurcated across VX-NET-01, VX-NET-02, VX-NET-03, and VX-NET-04. The database.yml must define a pooled connection string pointing to a resilient cluster, ensure the MaxPoolSize aligns with projected player counts, and confirm that failover options are enabled. In sync‑rules.yml the cross‑server trade rules and progression cap values must be alphabetized and validated against the game’s master data; any mismatch could lock inventory, causing in‑game economic bugs. health‑monitor.yml has to set alert thresholds that trigger automated backup jobs, service restarts, or network roll‑backs when CPU or latency metrics cross defined limits. Each of these files must pass syntax validation and be committed to the common configuration repository before the server infrastructure activates the VX‑NET network.
Always ensure that database.yml connections are healthy before scaling; a small lag in shared data can cascade across all your Hytale worlds.
